texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane  gas  separation  has  been  considered  as  an  interesting process  for  carbon  capture  from  natural  and  flue  gasses  due  to  its interesting  advantages  of  no-solvent  required,  easy  to  scale-up,  and lower footprint [1]. Commercially available membranes for gas separation are usually fabricated from polymeric materials since they are easy to process and lower cost. However, the performance of polymeric membranes  is  typically  limited  by  the  trade-off  between  membrane permeability and selectivity [2 -6]. Recently, there are significant efforts in finding new materials for addressing this issue. Among the materials, zeolite is the potential candidate since it has a well-defined pore structure  as  well  as  good  durability  for  high  operating  temperature  and pressure [7 -12].

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  this  work,  SAPO-34  membrane was successfully synthesized on silica support by an optimized secondary growth method which combines ageing and rubbing with the use of enhanced synthesis gel.  The enhanced synthesis gel was prepared by the addition of SAPO-34 crystals into the synthesis gel. Results of XRD and IR-ATR analysis of SAPO-34 seeds confirmed the formation of SAPO-34 crystal. SEM analysis showed that the crystal has cubic form and the size was about 200 -400 nm. The resultant crystals were then used for the preparation of SAPO-34 membrane on the inner surface of the tubular silica support. XRD and XRF analysis showed that SAPO34 layer was successfully formed on the membrane support with Si/Al ratio of 0.48 -0.49. SEM images showed that  the  optimized secondary growth method could produce continuous and defect-free SAPO-34 membrane (SAPO-34-M2). In contrast, membrane prepared by rubbing method with conventional synthesis gel (SAPO-34M1) displayed discontinued and defects layer. In single-gas permeation test, SAPO-34-M2 membrane showed two regions of N2 flux indicating the drastic effect of concentration polarization on N2 permeation at higher pressure. The  concentration  polarization  phenomenon  resulted  in  a  decrease  of  CO2/N2 selectivity  with  increasing transmembrane pressure. It was demonstrated by single-gas permeation test that the SAPO-34-M2 membrane could surpass the Robeson plot (2008) for CO2/N2  separation (permeance ¼ 2.01 � 10 6 mol/m 2 s  Pa;  the highest selectivity ¼ 53). The results showed that the prepared SAPO-34 membrane could be an alternative for CO2/N2 separation.

texts.orig: DDR [15,19,20], and SAPO-34 [21 -27]. Among those membranes, zeolites with 8-MR have pores that suitable for gas separation [28]. Zeolites and zeotype with 8-MR including SAPO-34, high silica CHA, and DDR have shown high separation performance for CO2/N2  separation application. SAPO-34 is attractive zeotype with 0.38 nm pore size which is suitable for achieving a high separation factor during CO2/N2  separation (CO2 kinetic diameter ¼ 0.330 nm; N2 kinetic diameter ¼ 0.364 nm) separation. Previous report demonstrated that SAPO-34 was highly stable at high temperature (over 100 � C) and high humidity environment  [29].  By  combining  those  favorable  characteristics,  SAPO-34 zeolite is potential membrane material for CO2/N2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, SAPO-34 membrane was synthesized on tubular pure silica.  The  membrane  was  fabricated  by  a  new  secondary  growth method.  The  new  secondary  growth  method  combined  the  rubbing method and ageing with enhanced synthesis gel as the synthesis solution. The enhanced synthesis gel was prepared by the addition of a small amount of SAPO-34 seeds into the synthesis gel. Gas separation properties of silica supported SAPO-34 membrane was evaluated by singlegas permeation test.

Gas separation properties of silica supported SAPO-34 membrane was evaluated by singlegas permeation test.

texts.orig: 2.1. Materials
texts.text: 2.1. Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fumed silica (Sigma Aldrich), Pseudo-boehmite (Sachem), H3PO4 (Merck),  were  used  as  sources  of  silica,  aluminum,  and  phosphate, respectively, in the preparation of SAPO-34 seeds and membrane. Tetraethylammonium  hydroxide  (TEAOH  from  Sachem)  was  used  as  a structural directing agent. Demineralized water was used in all experiment. Tubular silica (obtained from GDP Filter, Indonesia) with 8 mm inner diameter was used as the membrane support. Pure CO2  and N2 gasses (obtained from local suppliers, 99% purity) were used for gas permeation tests.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds
texts.text: 2.2. Synthesis of SAPO-34 seeds

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthesis gel for SAPO-34 seeds was prepared by mixing fumed silica, pseudo-boehmite, H3PO4 85 wt% aqueous solution, and TEAOH 35 wt% aqueous solution until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The molar composition of the synthesis gel was 1 Al2O3: 1 P2O5: 0.8 SiO2: 2 TEAOH: 55 H2O. Then, the resulting solution was poured into an autoclave to undergo hydrothermal synthesis at 200 � C for 48 h. SAPO-34 crystals were obtained by decantation method. The obtained crystals were  washed  several  times  by  distilled  water  and  dried  at  100 � C overnight. The SAPO-34 seeds were used for membrane preparation.

The molar composition of the synthesis gel was 1 Al2O3: 1 P2O5: 0.8 SiO2: 2 TEAOH: 55 H2O. Then, the resulting solution was poured into an autoclave to undergo hydrothermal synthesis at 200 � C for 48 h. SAPO-34 crystals were obtained by decantation method. The obtained crystals were  washed  several  times  by  distilled  water  and  dried  at  100 � C overnight. The SAPO-34 seeds were used for membrane preparation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Preparation of silica-supported SAPO-34 membrane
texts.text: 2.3. Preparation of silica-supported SAPO-34 membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The silica support was cleaned by boiling it with distilled water. The clean  support  was  dried  in  an  oven  overnight.  The  outside  of  the membrane  was  covered  using  Teflon  tape  to  avoid  SAPO-34  seed deposition. The support was seeded by rubbing SAPO-34 seeds on the inner surface of the support. One gram of SAPO-34 seeds were rubbed on inner surface of 10 cm silica support by using cotton swabs. Seeds were deposit on silica support at room condition. Membrane synthesis gel was made with the same molar composition of SAPO-34 seed synthesis gel. About 0.1 gr of SAPO-34 seed was mixed with the synthesis gel until a homogenous enhanced gel was obtained. The enhanced gel was aged for 24 h. Afterward, the suspension and seeded silica support were placed into an autoclave to undergo hydrothermal synthesis. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 200 � C for 24 h. The resultant membrane was washed by distilled water, dried at 100 � C overnight, and calcined at 400 � C for 8 h to remove the template (the structural directing agent). In this  work,  two  membranes  were  made.  The  first  one  used  rubbing method with conventional gel, while the second one was prepared by rubbing method, ageing, and enhanced gel. texts.orig: 2.4. Characterization
texts.text: 2.4. Characterization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SAPO-34 was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu-K α as incident beam ( λ ¼ 1.5418 Å). The morphology of SAPO-34 seeds and membrane surface were  characterized  by  Scanning  Electron  Microscopy  (SEM)  using  a Hitachi SU-3500 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Meanwhile, the morphology of cross-section SAPO-34 membrane was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) using a JEOL JSM6510A  microscope  at  an  accelerating  voltage  of  10  kV.  Before  SEM analysis, the sample was coated using gold in Ion Sputtering MCI000 Hitachi.  Bruker  Alpha spectrometer with attenuated total reflectance (ATR)  accessory  was  employed  to  collect  the  ATR-infrared  (ATR-IR) spectra of SAPO-34. The measurement was performed in the range of 4000 -500  cm 1 using  zinc  selenide  (ZnSe)  as  the  ATR  prism.  The composition of SAPO-34 was analyzed by X-ray fluorescence (XRF). The measurement was performed on a PANanalytical Axios mAX spectrometer.

texts.orig: Chabazite or CHA zeolite [33]. It is clear from the pattern that SAPO-34 crystal was synthesized successfully. ATR-IR spectra of SAPO-34 seeds is shown in Fig. 2 b. As shown in Fig. 2 b, SAPO-34 crystal exhibits IR bands which in agreement with the results reported in Ref. [34]. As shown in the IR-ATR spectra, peaks at 1213 cm 1  and 1005 cm 1 that correspond to P -O -Al asymmetric stretching band and P -O stretching band, respectively, appear after the crystalline sample formed [35]. The amorphous sample of SAPO-34 shows a broad peak of O -P -O stretching vibration  at  1100  cm 1 and  will  be  shifted  to  1120  cm 1 after  the crystalline  phase  formed  [35].  Also,  framework  characteristic  of SAPO-34 in T-O bending at 644 cm 1 (D6R), 571 cm 1 (PO4), and 532 cm 1 (Si/AlO4) appear at the IR-ATR spectra confirming the formation of pure SAPO-34 with CHA framework. Fig. 2 c shows the SEM image of SAPO-34 seeds or crystals. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the synthesized seed was formed  in  cubic  crystal.  The  size  of  SAPO-34  seeds  was  about  200 nm -400 nm. With those sizes, it is expected to obtain a membrane with smooth, continuous, and defect-free zeolite layer.

Fig. 2 c shows the SEM image of SAPO-34 seeds or crystals. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the synthesized seed was formed  in  cubic  crystal.  The  size  of  SAPO-34  seeds  was  about  200 nm -400 nm. With those sizes, it is expected to obtain a membrane with smooth, continuous, and defect-free zeolite layer.

texts.orig: Fig.  4  shows  the  SEM  images  of  SAPO-34-M1  and  SAPO-34-M2 membranes. The SEM images were taken from the surface of SAPO-34 membrane  layer  on  the  inner  surface  of  the  tubular  silica  support. SAPO-34 is classified as zeotype with Chabazite (CHA) structure which is characterized by rhombohedral shape. Such shape is observed in the SEM images. As shown in Fig. 4, the synthesized SAPO-34 membrane had almost no major defect (Fig. 4 b1 and b2). The size of SAPO-34 seeds on the membrane was about 200 -400 nm which was identical with the size of SAPO-34 seed. The resultant seed size is smaller than those obtained by previous work done by Li et al. [1]. Their preparation procedure of SAPO-34 membrane resulted in crystal size of 0.5 μ m -1 μ m. It was demonstrated by Carreon et al. [36]. that smaller crystals lead to a thinner membrane layer that decreased the mass resistance across the membrane. The smaller crystals might be attributed to the synthesis method used in this work that combined rubbing method, ageing, and enhanced  gel  (or  optimized  secondary  growth  method).  One  of  the important  parts  of  SAPO-34  synthesis  is  Si  incorporation  into  the framework of zeolite. Ageing period and the use of template were found to be the crucial steps for the crystallization of SAPO-34 [37]. Ageing increases the number of depolymerization of silica colloid which will react  with  Al(OH)4 -and PO4 3 to  produce  crystal  nuclei  of  SAPO-34. Before the crystallization temperature raised (at 0 h of synthesis time), preliminary structure unit similar to the framework of SAPO-34 was formed on which become the precursor of SAPO-34 crystal nuclei [37]. texts.orig: SAPO-34 layer can be prepared by dip-coating method. However, this  method  needs  special  heat  treatment  to  remove  hydroxypropyl cellulose which used for the colloidal stability in dip-coating solution [26]. The optimized secondary growth method requires no special heat treatment that can cause defects to the membrane layer. Heat treatment can cause defect because SAPO-34 has linear expansion coefficient from 0.5 � 10 6 to 16.7 � 10 6  K 1 [26]. Besides, the new method produced a more uniform layer than the use of the rubbing method only. It is quite difficult to maintain uniform seed distribution across the support surface by using the rubbing method only. SAPO-34-M1 (Fig. 4 a1 and a2) synthesized by rubbing method showed a layer with many holes across the support. Moreover, SAPO-34-M1 thickness cannot be identified  by  SEM  (Fig.  4  a1)  since  the  selective  layer  is  not  uniform.  In contrast, the addition of SAPO-34 crystals into the synthesis gel combined with rubbing method and ageing could produce the uniform layer of SAPO-34-M2 as shown by Fig. 4 b1 and b2. The thickness of SAPO-34 selective layer is about 1.7 μ m. This indicates that the new method can be used to produce a thin and continuous SAPO-34 layer on the silica support  effectively.  Such  characteristics  are  important  for  obtaining both high permeability and selectivity.

texts.orig: SAPO-5 or AlPO-34 framework. Meanwhile, the higher aluminum content will lead to higher calcination temperature, which will create more defects on membrane selective layer [26]. However, too higher Si content will yield in lower adsorption capacity. As reported by Li et al. [39], 0.8 decrease in the Si/Al ratio resulted in 50% increase of adsorbed CO2. Furthermore,  too  high  Si  content  will  result  in  non-uniformity  of  Si concentration in the crystal from the core to the surface [40]. As shown in Table 1, the membrane layer had the silica content of more than 0.15. It resulted in pure SAPO-34 and lower calcination temperature needed. XRF analysis  of  SAPO-34  membrane proves  that  the  Si/Al  ratio  was about 0.49. With this composition, the membrane is expected to pose high CO2 adsorption capacity, pure SAPO-34 framework, and well-distributed Si content in the membrane structure.

texts.orig: 3.3. CO2/N2 separation
texts.text: 3.3. CO2/N2 separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of SAPO-34 membranes are depicted in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, CO2 and N2 fluxes increase with  increasing  transmembrane  pressure.  It  is  obvious  since  higher pressure results  in  higher  concentration difference  between feed and permeate side leading to higher mass transfer or flux. An interesting result was observed in N2 flux of SAPO-34-M2 (shown in Fig. 5 b ' ). There are two regions in N2 flux vs trans-membrane pressure curve of SAPO34-M2. N2  flux  is  more  pronounced  in  region  II  than  in  region  I.  It might imply that the concentration polarization occurred on the membrane  surface  [25]  giving  more  drastic  effect  on  N2  flux  at  higher trans-membrane pressure. It is well known that higher pressure results in higher gas concentration. It means more N2 concentration at the interface of membrane surface/bulk gas since N2  is rejected by the membrane.  As  a  result,  N2  flux  is  increased  further.  Unfortunately,  this phenomenon leads to decreasing CO2/N2  selectivity (Fig. 5 and b). A dramatic  decrease  of  selectivity  was  observed  in  the  region  II  of SAPO-34-M2 membrane.

texts.orig: The performance of SAPO-34-M2 with silica support and optimized secondary growth shows the higher performance of CO2 permeance than alumina supported membranes (see Table 2). This result confirms that silica  support  is  suitable  for  the  fabrication  of  SAPO-34  membrane which may be attributed to its aforementioned features. The growth of SAPO-34 on porous support follows the rule of gel conversion mechanism so the appropriate solution support and solution circumstance are important parameters that should be maintained carefully [37]. Otherwise, SAPO-34-M1 with silica support and rubbing method (only) shows lower performance due to defects formation across the membrane. The formation of defects may be associated with the uneven distribution of zeolite crystal on the support. Results of this study show that the new method of optimized secondary growth could address the issue. This is also supported by the data presented in Fig. 6. Permeance and selected selectivity of SAPO-34-M1 and SAPO-34-M2 were plotted and compared with other membrane materials. As can be seen in Fig. 6, SAPO-34-M2 could surpass the upper bound (Robeson 2008) indicating the potential application of this membrane for CO2/N2  separation, especially at relatively low pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, SAPO-34 membrane was synthesized on tubular pure silica support by optimized secondary growth method. Results of XRD and  IR-ATR  analysis  of  SAPO-34  seeds  confirmed  the  formation  of SAPO-34 crystal. SEM analysis showed that the crystal was cubic and the size was about 200 -400 nm. The resultant crystals were then used for the preparation of SAPO-34 membrane on the inner surface of the support. XRD and XRF analysis showed that SAPO-34 layer was successfully formed on the membrane support with Si/Al ratio of 0.48 -0.49. SEM images showed that the use of optimized secondary growth method (by using enhanced synthesis gel and combined rubbing/ageing method) could produce continuous and defect-free SAPO-34 membrane (SAPO34-M2) compared to rubbing method only with conventional synthesis gel  (SAPO-34-M1). In  single-gas  permeation  test,  SAPO-34-M2  membrane showed two regions for N2  flux indicating the drastic effect of concentration polarization on N2  permeation at higher pressure. The concentration polarization phenomenon resulted in a decrease of CO2/ N2  selectivity with increasing transmembrane pressure. It was demonstrated by single-gas permeation test that the SAPO-34-M2 membrane could  surpass  the  Robeson  plot  (2008)  for  CO2/N2 separation  (permeance ¼ 2.01 ( � 10 6 mol/m 2 s Pa; the highest selectivity ¼ 53). The results showed that the prepared SAPO-34 membrane could be a potential alternative for CO2/N2 separation.
texts.text: In this work, SAPO-34 membrane was synthesized on tubular pure silica support by optimized secondary growth method. Results of XRD and  IR-ATR  analysis  of  SAPO-34  seeds  confirmed  the  formation  of SAPO-34 crystal. SEM analysis showed that the crystal was cubic and the size was about 200 -400 nm. The resultant crystals were then used for the preparation of SAPO-34 membrane on the inner surface of the support. XRD and XRF analysis showed that SAPO-34 layer was successfully formed on the membrane support with Si/Al ratio of 0.48 -0.49. SEM images showed that the use of optimized secondary growth method (by using enhanced synthesis gel and combined rubbing/ageing method) could produce continuous and defect-free SAPO-34 membrane (SAPO34-M2) compared to rubbing method only with conventional synthesis gel  (SAPO-34-M1). In  single-gas  permeation  test,  SAPO-34-M2  membrane showed two regions for N2  flux indicating the drastic effect of concentration polarization on N2  permeation at higher pressure. The concentration polarization phenomenon resulted in a decrease of CO2/ N2  selectivity with increasing transmembrane pressure. It was demonstrated by single-gas permeation test that the SAPO-34-M2 membrane could  surpass  the  Robeson  plot  (2008)  for  CO2/N2 separation  (permeance ¼ 2.01 ( � 10 6 mol/m 2 s Pa; the highest selectivity ¼ 53). The results showed that the prepared SAPO-34 membrane could be a potential alternative for CO2/N2 separation.
